passages.explanation: Paul looks straight at the Sanhedrin and says: my brothers, I have fulfilled my duty to God in all good conscience to this day. The high priest Ananias orders those near Paul to strike him on the mouth. Paul fires back: God will strike you, you whitewashed wall! You sit there to judge me according to the law, yet you yourself violate the law by commanding that I be struck. Those standing nearby rebuke Paul: how dare you insult God's high priest? Paul pulls back: brothers, I didn't realise he was the high priest, for it is written, do not speak evil about the ruler of your people. Whether Paul genuinely didn't know or is being sarcastic is debated — Ananias was widely regarded as corrupt.

Paul notices that the Sanhedrin is composed of both Sadducees and Pharisees. He calls out: brothers, I am a Pharisee, descended from Pharisees. I'm on trial because of my hope in the resurrection of the dead. This immediately divides the assembly — Sadducees deny the resurrection, angels, and spirits, while Pharisees affirm all three. A violent argument erupts. Some Pharisees even defend Paul: we find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or angel has spoken to him? The dispute becomes so fierce that the commander fears Paul will be torn to pieces and orders soldiers to take him by force back to the barracks. That night the Lord stands near Paul and says: take courage! As you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome. The promise of Rome is now direct — Paul will get there.

The next morning, more than forty Jews form a conspiracy, binding themselves with an oath not to eat or drink until they've killed Paul. They tell the chief priests and elders to ask the commander to bring Paul to the council again, supposedly for further questioning — they'll ambush and kill him on the way. But Paul's nephew — his sister's son — hears about the plot and goes to the barracks to warn Paul. Paul sends the young man to the commander. The commander takes the boy aside, hears the report privately, and tells him not to tell anyone else. The conspiracy is foiled by a family member whose name we never learn. Providence works through ordinary people with ordinary access.

The commander acts decisively. He orders two centurions to get ready two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen to escort Paul to Caesarea that night — to Governor Felix. He writes a letter explaining the situation: this man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed. He intervened because he learned Paul was a Roman citizen. He found the accusations related to Jewish law, with no charge deserving death or imprisonment. When informed of a plot against Paul's life, he sent him to Felix and told the accusers to present their case there. The soldiers escort Paul by night to Antipatris, and the next day the cavalry continues to Caesarea. Felix reads the letter, learns Paul is from Cilicia, and says he'll hear the case when the accusers arrive. Paul is kept under guard in Herod's palace. Jerusalem is no longer safe — the Roman justice system becomes Paul's protection.
